coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen – Doped carbon dots from Lantana camara berries for effective detection of lead(II) and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Bandi, Rajkumar
Dadigala, Ramakrishna
Gangapuram, Bhagavanth Reddy
Guttena, Veerabhadram
coredata.dc:description: Here, we developed a simple green approach for the synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) using Lantana camara berries. Optical and physicochemical properties of as synthesized NCDs were extensively studied by using various analytical techniques. NCDs exhibited bright fluorescence with Quantum yield as high as 33.15%, which is stable to various effects like heat, pH, ionic strength and continuous irradiation. Furthermore, the NCDs presented highly selective and sensitive fluorescence response towards Pb2+ which explored their potential to serve as a label-free fluorescent probe for the effective detection of Pb2+. As developed NCDs based probe exhibited a great linear response (R2 =0.998) towards Pb2+ in the concentration range of 0–200nM with a detection limit of 9.64nM. The probe further presented high precision without any interference and was successfully applied for the detection of Pb2+ in the real water and human sera (serum and urine) samples. Cytotoxicity studies on both cancerous (human breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7) and normal (Human embryonic kidney HEK-293) cell lines revealed their excellent biocompatibility. With their low cytotoxicity, strong fluorescence and excitation-dependent emission, NCDs were successfully applied as multi-colour bioimaging agents and Pb2+ detection capabilities were further evaluated in live cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence
Lead detection
Bioimaging
Intracellular detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cellular Imaging and Intracellular Detection of Pb2+
Analysis of Real Water and Biological Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of NCDs (b) HR-TEM (c) particle size distribution histogram.
XPS spectra of NCDs: (a) survey spectrum (b) C 1s spectrum (c) N 1s spectrum (d) O 1s spectrum.

(a) Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs, inset: photographs of NCDs aqueous solution under day light (left) and under UV light (right). (b) Emission spectra of NCDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence response of NCDs (a) in the presence of various metal ions (b) in the presence of increasing concentration of Pb2+. (c) The relationship between relative fluorescence response (F0/F) of NCDs and the concentration of Pb2+ from 0 to 200nM (d) Fluorescence decay curves of NCDs in the presence and absence of Pb2+.

(a) Fluorescence response (F/F0) of NCDs towards Pb2+ in the presence of other metal ions (b) Fluorescence response (F/F0) of NCDs towards various amino acids (black bars) & subsequent addition of Pb2+ (red bars). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal microscopic images of MCF-7 cells: control cell, cells treated with 0.2mgmL−1 of NCDs for 6h and cells further treated with Pb2+ for 1h, images under three excitation wavelengths of 405nm (blue), 488nm (green) and 561nm (red). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic representation for the synthesis and applications of NCDs.
Recoveries for the determination of Pb2+ in real water and human sera samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Standard deviation of six replicate values.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen – Doped carbon dots from Lantana camara berries for effective detection of lead(II) and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Osmania University, Hyderabad 500007, Telangana State, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, PG Center Wanaparthy, Palamuru University, Mahabub Nagar 509001, Telangana State, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we developed a simple green approach for the synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) using Lantana camara berries. Optical and physicochemical properties of as synthesized NCDs were extensively studied by using various analytical techniques. NCDs exhibited bright fluorescence with Quantum yield as high as 33.15%, which is stable to various effects like heat, pH, ionic strength and continuous irradiation. Furthermore, the NCDs presented highly selective and sensitive fluorescence response towards Pb2+ which explored their potential to serve as a label-free fluorescent probe for the effective detection of Pb2+. As developed NCDs based probe exhibited a great linear response (R2 =0.998) towards Pb2+ in the concentration range of 0–200nM with a detection limit of 9.64nM. The probe further presented high precision without any interference and was successfully applied for the detection of Pb2+ in the real water and human sera (serum and urine) samples. Cytotoxicity studies on both cancerous (human breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7) and normal (Human embryonic kidney HEK-293) cell lines revealed their excellent biocompatibility. With their low cytotoxicity, strong fluorescence and excitation-dependent emission, NCDs were successfully applied as multi-colour bioimaging agents and Pb2+ detection capabilities were further evaluated in live cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), during recent years, have emerged as a brand-new category of fluorescent nanomaterials as a result of their numerous fascinating properties [1]. Moreover, they were successfully applied in various fields like sensing, bioimaging, drug/gene delivery, catalysis, and optoelectronic devices [2,3]. With their outstanding properties like high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, functionalizability and photo stability, they are considered to be superior over conventional fluorescent materials like semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes. They have also been proven as promising candidates for the preparation of fluorescent chemosensors and biosensors for the detection of various analytes [4]. To date, several approaches such as laser ablation, arc discharge, thermal treatment, electrochemical oxidation, combustion, ultra-sonication, hydrothermal and microwave treatment are developed to synthesize CDs [1]. Research is still focused on improving the properties of CDs like poor fluorescence Quantum Yields (QY) and their low sensitivity towards the analyte. Many researchers have made efforts to improve the functionality of CDs, with post surface modification and intrinsic heteroatom doping being two major ways of controlling their chemical, optical and electrical properties [5]. Surface modification with polymeric molecules was at first adopted to improve QY of CDs, and since the difficulties in removing the excess of polymers, small organic molecules were employed later [6]. However, post surface modification is tedious and time-consuming as the purification of resultant functionalized CDs is unavoidable and the stability of CDs is a major concern in the following applications. Therefore, intrinsic heteroatom doping is considered to be an effective approach [7].

Among various elements, doping with N is the most widely used strategy and it can effectively tune the intrinsic properties of CDs, including their surface chemical reactivities, electronic properties and optical properties [8]. Though N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with high QY can be obtained from small organic molecular precursors, high cost and toxicity of these chemicals restrict their wider applications [9]. Hence, the usage of nitrogen containing natural precursors is considered to be an attractive alternative as it offers inexpensive production via green synthesis. Many researchers reported the synthesis of NCDs from several natural precursors such as milk [10], grass [11], seaweed [12], mushroom [13], peanut shells [14], silk [15], Prunus avium fruit [16], soy milk [17] and prawn shells [18]. Although these NCDs have been successfully employed in sensing and imaging applications, they suffer from major drawbacks like low QY and production yield, which limits their practical application. Hence, the development of simple, economical and green strategies for the production of highly fluorescent NCDs on large scale is highly desired.

While technological advancements using metal ions have improved the quality of life, on the other hand they have deteriorated the quality of all the environmental segments [19]. One such example is that the use of lead in batteries, pigments, anti-corrosion coatings, alloys etc., as a part of our daily life, which is considered to be hazardous when disposed in the environment [20]. Lead accumulated inside the body is highly difficult to be removed either through chemical or biological processes [21]. Accumulation of lead can generate high blood pressure and kidney disorders and have a severe impact on children between the ages of one and five years [22,23]. Concentrations of Pb2+ higher than 5μM in the blood can lead to anemia, developmental disorders, reproductive and nervous system dysfunctions etc. Too high concentrations of Pb2+ can even result in death [24]. Currently, Pb2+ concentrations in environmental and biological samples are determined using Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [25], anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) [26], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [27] etc. However, the requirement of expensive and sophisticated instrumentation, complex pre-treatment procedures and time consumption limit these techniques to be used widely. Hence, there is a great need to develop simple, rapid and cost-effective methodologies for the effective detection of Pb2+. In view of this, fluorescence based methods are highly attractive because of their simplicity, ease of operation and high sensitivity [28].

In the present work, we report a simple green methodology for the production of highly fluorescent NCDs with QY as high as 33.15%, using Lantana camara berries and ethylene diamine (EDN) as carbon and nitrogen source respectively. Lantana camara is regarded both as a notorious weed and a popular ornamental garden plant growing in tropical, sub-tropical and temperate regions [29]. It has found various uses in folk medicine in many parts of the world [30]. The ripe blue-black berries are edible, sweet and also eaten in some tropical countries. In general, berries are considered as an abundant source of several carbon-rich chemical components like polyphenols, glycosides and carbohydrates. As these berries are inexpensive, readily available and contain carbon rich chemical components they can be best used as carbon source for the synthesis of NCDs. Optical and physicochemical properties of as synthesized NCDs were thoroughly investigated by using various analytical techniques. The presence of various polar functional groups on the surface of NCDs made them potential candidates for the detection of Pb2+ ions. Detection capabilities were further evaluated in real water, human sera (serum and urine) samples and even under intra-cellular conditions as well. Further, with their low cytotoxicity, they were successfully employed as bioimaging agents.

Ripen fruits of Lantana camara were collected from our University garden. Human serum samples were obtained from Osmania General Hospital. Human urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double distilled (DD) water was used throughout the experiment.

5g of ripen blue black berries of Lantana camara were first washed thoroughly under running tap water and distilled water. Then they were processed into juice using a blender with 50mL of DD water. Then the mixture was filtered through Whatman filter paper to get a clear juice. Then 20mL of filtrate along with 80μL of EDA was transferred to a 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 3h in an oven. The obtained dark brown solution of NCDs was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22μm membrane filter to remove insoluble and non-fluorescent black particles. NCDs solution was further purified by dialyzing against double distilled water using a dialysis tubing (MWCO: 3500Da) for 24h. At last, the product was dried by a freeze dryer and certain amount of dried product was dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare a desired solution (1mgmL−1) and stored in a refrigerator for further use.
Details about the characterization of NCDS are given in Supplementary information.
Quantum yields of all the NCDs samples were determined by comparative method using Quinine sulphate (QS) as standard (Ф=54%). Complete details are given in Supplementary information.

All metal ion solutions were prepared from their respective salts using deionized water. 1mL of NCDs solution (0.16mgmL−1) was mixed with 1mL of deionized water and the fluorescence intensity of this sample at λem =450nm (λex =360nm) was taken as F0. In the same way, 1mL of NCDs solution was mixed with 1mL of different metal ion solutions having 20μM concentration. The samples were shaken well and incubated for 2min then the fluorescence intensity was measured and taken as F. For the quantitative determination of Pb2+, 1mL of NCDs solution was mixed with 1mL of Pb2+ solution having varying concentrations.

In vitro cytotoxicity of NCDs was evaluated on both cancerous (MCF-7) and noncancerous (HEK) cell lines using standard MTT Assay. The cells were cultured in 96-well tissue culture plates with DME medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin solution in an incubator (5% CO2, 37°C) at a density of 1×104 cells per well. After attachment, the cells were incubated with medium containing different concentrations of NCDs for 48h and the medium from each well was removed after incubation and the cells were washed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS). A fresh medium containing 10μL of 5mgmL−1 solution of MTT was added to each well and incubated for 4h. Then the medium was replaced with 150μL of DMSO followed by shaking for 10min at room temperature. The optical density (OD) of each well was recorded at 490nm using a multimode microplate reader (Biotek, Cytation3). The untreated cells were used as controls for calculating the relative percentage cell viability from the following equation:
Cell viability
%
=
OD treated
/
OD control
× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular Imaging and Intracellular Detection of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular imaging potential and intracellular Pb2+ detection capability of NCDs was tested using MCF-7 Cells. MCF-7 cells were cultivated in 6-well culture plates with DMEM containing 10% Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin solution in an incubator (5% CO2, 37°C) until the cell count reaches to 105 per well. Then the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing 0.2mgmL−1 NCDs and further incubated for 6h. After that, the cells were washed thrice with PBS to remove extracellular NCDs and imaged under a Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (Zeiss LSM 510 Meta) at laser excitations of 405, 488 and 561nm. Subsequently, cellular imaging experiments were performed by further incubating the NCDs treated cells with 10μM Pb(NO3)2 for 1h, to evaluate the intracellular detection ability of NCDs.

In this paper, we report a simple hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of NCDs using Lantana camara fruit juice as carbon precursor and EDN as the nitrogen source. Synthesis and application of NCDs is represented in Scheme 1 . Lantana camara berries are known to contain carbohydrates, glycosides and flavonoids [31]. Glycosides are known to produce sugars upon treatment with alkali. Hence, the hydrothermal treatment of fruit juice along with ethylene diamine will first result in the hydrolysis, dehydration and decomposition of carbohydrates and glycosides to produce small molecules which then undergo polymerization, aromatization and carbonization processes to produces NCDs. In order to get highly fluorescent carbon dots, synthetic conditions like the amount of EDN used, reaction time and reaction temperature were optimized by taking QY as the parameter. As shown in Fig. S1, temperature of 180°C and 3h of reaction time using 80μL of EDN resulted in NCDs with QY as high as 33.15%. Under the optimal synthetic conditions, the production yield of NCDs was calculated to be 7.84% as 392mg of product was obtained from 5g of Lantana camara berries. We made a comparison of synthetic conditions, QY and production yield of NCDs obtained from various natural precursors with our method (Table S1), and it clearly presented that our method is superior over several previous reports in terms of the mild reaction conditions employed and high QY & production yield achieved.

The physicochemical properties of NCDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, Raman, EDX, XPS, FTIR and zeta potential. The Morphology, size and microstructure of NCDs prepared under optimal synthetic conditions were assessed by TEM. Fig. 1a showing TEM images of NCDs demonstrate that NCDs were monodispersed with near spherical morphology and size distribution in the range of 3 to 8nm with an average particle size of 5±3nm (Fig. 1c). The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) revealed lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 3.4Å, corresponding to the interspacing of (002) plane of graphite [32]. Powder XRD pattern (Fig. S2a) of NCDs exhibit a broad peak at around 24.38°, corresponding to (002) lattice plane of graphite, indicating a disordered structure of carbon framework with an amorphous nature of surface structure [33]. The Raman spectra of NCDs (Fig. S2b) show two broad peaks each centred at 1378 and 1548cm−1 attributing to the disordered D and crystalline G bands of graphite respectively. SEM-EDX results (Fig. S3) showed that the elemental composition of NCDs is C (66.18%), N (12.63%) and O (21.19%).

To get a clear information regarding the surface states and chemical composition of NCDs, XPS and FTIR analysis were performed. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2a) of NCDs exhibits 3 peaks each at 285, 399 and 530eV, which are ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s signals respectively. The high resolution C1s (Fig. 2b) reveals that carbons present in three different chemical environments corresponding to sp2 (CC)/sp3 (CC and CH) at 285.2eV, CN/CO at 287.6eV and CO/CN at 289.1eV [34]. High resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) indicates the presence of CNC (at 399.6eV) and NH (at 401.3eV) chemical bonds, which concludes the successful incorporation of N into the carbon lattice [35]. O1s spectrum can be resolved into two distinct peaks each at 530.1 and 532.6eV ascribed to the presence of CO and COH/COC functional groups respectively [36]. As shown in Fig. S4a, the FT-IR spectrum exhibits a broad band in the range of 3600 to 3100cm−1 characteristic for amine NH2 and hydroxyl OH functional groups. Peak at 2924cm− 1 can be ascribed to CH stretching. A strong band at of 1658cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching frequency and peaks at 1325 and 1423cm−1 can be attributed to the aromatic CC stretching frequencies. Further, the peaks at 1086 and 1157cm−1 are assigned to the CO and CN stretching frequencies respectively. The existence of hydroxyl and carboxylate groups on the surface of NCDs was further confirmed by a negative zeta potential value (−11mV) (Fig. S4b). All the above results demonstrate that the surface of NCDs is coated with polar functional groups imparting excellent solubility and facilitating their coordination with Pb2+ ions.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectral studies were performed to gain an insight into the optical properties of as synthesized NCDs. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3a, NCDs aqueous solution is pale yellow and transparent under day light and emits bright blue fluorescence under UV light. In the UV–Vis spectra of NCDs aqueous solution (Fig. 3a), the peak at 285nm could be ascribed to the π–π* transitions of aromatic SP2 domains and the peak at 356nm was associated with the n–π* transition and the formation of excited defect surface states, which could lead to strong fluorescence [33]. Fig. 3b depicts the fluorescence spectra of NCDs at various excitation wavelengths, which clearly indicates the excitation dependent emission. With a gradual increase in the excitation wavelength from 320 to 480nm, a clear red shift in the emission peak was observed which is accompanied by a change in the emission intensity. Maximum emission was observed at 450nm with the excitation wavelength of 360nm exhibiting a stoke shift of 90nm. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the strong emission peak is about 90nm, which is in consistent with the previous reports and further confirming their narrow size distribution [37,38]. This excitation dependent emission property is considered to be the versatile characteristic of carbon dots making them a fluorescent probe for multicolour imaging. Further, the fluorescence QY of NCDs was calculated using QS as the reference and found to be 33.15% (Fig. S5), which is on a higher side in comparison to the CDs prepared from various precursors (Table S1). Nitrogen doping and the existence of electron-rich nitrogen and oxygen containing functional groups generally known as excellent auxochromes, might be the reasons for this high QY [39].

To evaluate the potential application of NCDs as a fluorescent biomarker in the practical biological environment, we investigated the fluorescence stability of NCDs aqueous solution. Photo-stability studies revealed that only a negligible decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. S6a) even after continuous irradiation for 2h. Further, the effect of ionic strength was also examined by employing different concentrations of KCl. As represented in Fig. S6b, NCDs exhibited a high tolerance to salt concentrations up to 2M, which further extends their applicability to similar ion-rich biological environments. Fluorescence emission of NCDs was measured at different pH values by adjusting the pH using 0.1N HCl and 0.1N NaOH solutions. It was observed that with the change in the pH, fluorescence intensity changed with a constant emission wavelength and strong fluorescence emission was observed in the pH range of 5–9 (Fig. S7). Thermal stability was tested by heating the NCDs aqueous solution in a sealed container at 90°C. As shown in the Fig. S8a, even after heating for 90min, no significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed indicating their higher thermal stability. The shelf life of NCDs was assessed by storing under two different conditions: 1) at room temperature (30°C) and 2) in a refrigerator (5°C). After 60days of storage, no changes were observed in both the samples (Fig. S8b), representing their good stability and high shelf life.

Fluorescence response of NCDs towards various metal ions such as Na+, K+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ca2+ and Fe3+ was investigated to explore their capability of acting as a fluorescent probe for metal ion detection. As depicted in Fig. 4a, among various metal ions, only Pb2+ caused a significant fluorescence quenching implying that the NCDs could be employed as a turn-off chemo-sensor for the selective detection of Pb2+. In the process of developing NCDs based fluorescent probe for the efficient detection of Pb2+, we further optimized the experimental conditions such as the concentration of NCDs, incubation time and pH of the solution. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs with varying concentrations was recorded and represented in Fig. S9a. As shown in figure, emission intensity of NCDs is directly proportional to its concentration in the range of 0–0.08mgmL−1, while it exhibited inverse relation above this concentration and maximum emission was observed at 0.08mgmL−1 which is taken as optimal. At high concentrations, self-absorption quenching is often observed in fluorescent materials, including carbon-based materials which result in the decreased fluorescence emission with increase in concentration [40]. Kinetic experiments (Fig. S9b) revealed that within 2min of interaction with Pb2+ (200nM), the emission intensity of NCDs decreased to a constant value which doesn't change even after 1h. This indicates that the reaction between the surface functional groups of NCDs and Pb2+ is very fast and stable suggesting a promising application for quick sensing of Pb2+ without any strict time control. As the NCDs exhibited pH dependent emission, it is important to study the effect of pH on the Pb2+ detection capabilities also. Relative fluorescence response (F0/F) of NCDs towards Pb2+ (200nM) at different pH values is represented in Fig. S9c. As shown in the figure, NCDs exhibited good fluorescence response in the pH range of 5–9, which is an advantageous and interesting feature, as most of the water and biological samples lie in this pH range. Maximum response was observed at pH7, which is used as optimum pH for the assay. Under acidic pH, protonation of surface functional groups of NCDs takes place leading to a decrease in their coordination with Pb2+ while partial hydrolysis of Pb2+ occurs under alkaline conditions [41].

Sensitivity of NCDs based detection system was examined by measuring their fluorescence response towards different concentrations of Pb2+ in the range of 0 to 3000nM, under the optimized experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4b, with increase in the Pb2+ concentration, a steady decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 450nm was observed, indicating their sensitivity towards Pb2+. Relative fluorescence response as (F0/F) as a function of Pb2+ concentration is shown in Fig. S10. The quenching efficiency (F0/F) displayed a good linear correlation (R2 =0.998) over a concentration range of 0–200nM (Fig. 4c), where F0 and F were the emission intensities of NCDs at 450nm (λex =360nm) in the absence and presence of Pb2+ respectively. The linear regression equation for the same can be represented as.
F 0
/ F = 0.00311
Pb
2 +
nM
+ 1.001

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by using the equation 3σ/m (where σ is the standard deviation of blank signal (n=6) and m is the slope of the linear fit) and was found to be 9.64nM. We made a comparison of analytical performance of the present method with several previous reports (Table S2) and it clearly presented that our method is advantageous. Reliability of the present detection system was evaluated by performing inter-day and intra-day precision experiments. Precision was expressed as relative standard deviation (RSD %) of repeated measurements (n=6) performed by using a fixed concentration of Pb2+ (100nM) under the optimal conditions. Intra-day precision was measured by performing repeated analysis on the same day and found to be 2.12%. Inter-day precision was determined by performing the analysis on six consecutive days and found to be 2.23%.

Anti–interference ability study is one of the most important evaluations for a detection system for practical applications [42]. Interference of various other metal ions was studied by recording the emission spectra in the co-presence Pb2+ and other metal ions (each at a concentration 10 times higher than that of Pb2+). As shown in Fig. 5a, no significant change in the fluorescence response was observed in the co-presence of Pb2+ with other ions, compared to that of Pb2+ alone. Amino acids in the biological system were also capable of causing potential interference with their ability to interact with a lot of metal cations. Hence, we further examined the effect various amino acids (each at a concentration of 100μM) on the fluorescence emission of NCDs and quenching efficiency of Pb2+. As shown in Fig. 5b, the interference caused by these amino acids was also negligible. These results indicated the highly selective nature of the present detection system towards Pb2+ and encouraged us to explore their applicability in the analysis of real water and biological samples.

According to several previous reports, different types of NCDs can specifically interact with different metal ions through oxygen or amino functional groups on the NCDs surface and lead to fluorescence quenching; unfortunately, the exact mechanism is still not clear [43]. However, the possible mechanism of sensing can be predicted by carefully investigating the fluorescence quenching behaviour. Static quenching and dynamic/collisional quenching are the two main forms of fluorescence quenching. Static quenching involves the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between the quencher and the fluorophore, whereas dynamic quenching involves the transfer of electron from the excited state fluorophore to ground state quencher [40]. As molecular contact is the key requirement for both static and dynamic quenching processes to take place, the selective fluorescence response can be ascribed to the high binding affinity of the surface functional groups of NCDs towards a specific metal ion (Pb2+) over others.

To explore the possible quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching behaviour was studied by applying standard Stern–Volmer equation.
F 0
/ F =
K sv
C + 1
where, F0 and F were the emission intensities of NCDs at 450nm (λex =360nm) in the absence and presence of Pb2+ respectively, Ksv is Stern–Volmer quenching constant and C is the concentration of the quencher (Pb2+).

As represented in the Fig. 4c, the Stern–Volmer plot followed a good linear trend over the Pb2+ concentration range of 0 to 200nM and the Stern–Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) was found to be 3.11×106 mol−1 dm3. Dynamic or static quenching can be discriminated by studying the temperature dependence of quenching constant. Stern–Volmer plots obtained at 3 different temperatures (Fig. S11a) clearly indicated that the quenching constant is directly proportional to temperature. It is well known that the collision frequency increases with temperature (number of collisions between Pb2+ and NCDs increases), hence the raise in the quenching constant with increase in temperature can be attributed to its collision/dynamic nature [40]. As shown in the Fig. S11b no obvious change in the absorption peak position of NCDs is observed even after the addition of Pb2+, which further suggested that there was no static intermediate formation between NCDs and Pb2+. To get deep inside the quenching mechanism, time resolved fluorescence decay analysis was performed using TCSPC method. As shown in the Fig. 4d the average fluorescence lifetime of NCDs decreased significantly (from 4.76ns to 2.79ns) after the addition of Pb2+, which further confirmed the dynamic quenching. From the above results, we proposed a plausible detection mechanism according to which the high binding affinity of the surface functional groups causes NCDs to interact selectively with Pb2+ and forms a molecular contact (required for the quenching process to occur) first, which then facilitates the nonradiative electron transfer from the excited state of NCDs to the vacant d orbitals of Pb2+ and results in the quenching of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Real Water and Biological Samples
ce:section.ce:para: Impressed by their fascinating analytical features like high sensitivity, selectivity, good precision and anti-interference ability, the analytical performance of the NCDs based Pb2+ detection system is further evaluated in real samples. For this analysis, 2 different water samples (tap, and pond water) and human sera (serum and urine) samples were used. Water samples were centrifuged and filtered through 0.45μ filter membrane to remove particulate matter, while human sera samples were diluted 100 times with PBS and no other treatment needed. Then the samples were spiked with different amounts of Pb2+, analysed by the similar procedure and the recoveries obtained from the standard curve (Fig. 4c) were provided in Table 1 . As shown in the table, great recoveries (98.40 to 102.10%) with decent RSD values (<2) were obtained, demonstrating the potential of the present sensing system for the detection of Pb2+ in environmental as well as biological samples. Human exposure to lead is preliminarily assessed by measuring its concentrations in the body fluids like blood and urine [44]. The current work offers a robust detection system with the ability to detect Pb2+ in human sera samples without any inference. Hence, this method could be applied in the future for the rapid and inexpensive detection of lead in the body fluids of living organism exposed to lead poisoning. Furthermore, the proposed sensor is validated by detecting the Pb2+ content in waste water sample and the results were compared with that of standard Atomic absorption spectroscopy. As shown in the Table 1, the results are in well agreement with the AAS results.

With the superior optical properties like bright and stable fluorescence with high QY (33.15%), NCDs can serve as potential bio-labelling agents. However, high biocompatibility is the key requirement to meet such application [45]. Hence to investigate the biocompatibility of NCDs MTT assay was performed, in which the inherent cytotoxicity of NCDs was evaluated by measuring the relative viabilities of normal (Human embryonic kidney HEK-293) and cancerous (Human breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7) cells treated with different concentrations of NCDs. As shown in Fig. S12, NCDs have not exhibited any significant toxicity on both cancer and noncancerous cell lines and over 90% of viability was observed even after 48h of their incubation with NCDs (in the concentration range of 0.1 to 1mgmL−1). These results suggest that the NCDs are highly biocompatible and can serve as a suitable and safe probe for both in-vitro and in-vivo applications.

To reveal the potential applications for bio-labelling, cellular imaging capability of NCDs was evaluated using MCF-7 cells. MCF-7 cells after incubation with NCDs (0.2mgmL−1) for 6h, imaged under a confocal laser scanning microscope. As shown in Fig. 6 , the cells incubated with NCDs were brightly illuminated with blue, green and red colour upon the excitation at 408, 488 and 561nm respectively, while there is no detectable fluorescence for control cells (without NCDs) under the same exposure conditions.

Encouraged by their high biocompatibility, cellular imaging capability and excellent selectivity to Pb2+ under complex environmental and biological samples, we further investigated their applicability for monitoring intracellular Pb2+. Exogenous Pb2+ is introduced into the NCDs pre-treated MCF cells by incubating them with 10μM Pb2+ for 1h and the cells were imaged under the confocal microscope. As expected the microscopic images (Fig. 6) presented very weak intracellular fluorescence, which indicates that the present nanosensor can serve as an effective probe for intracellular Pb2+ sensing.

Here, we have successfully developed a simple green methodology for the synthesis of highly fluorescent NCDs (QY 33.15%) using Lantana camara berries. Optical and physicochemical properties of as synthesized NCDs were thoroughly characterized using various analytical techniques. NCDs exhibited strong fluorescence, which is stable to various effects like heat, pH, ionic strength and continuous irradiation. With their highly selective and sensitive fluorescence response towards Pb2+, NCDs based label free fluorescent probe for the effective detection of Pb2+ was developed. The probe further presented high precision without any interference and was successfully applied for the detection of Pb2+ in the real water and human sera (serum and urine) samples. With their low cytotoxicity, strong fluorescence and excitation dependent emission, NCDs were successfully applied for multi-colour imaging and intracellular detection of Pb2+ also. All these fascinating properties like high stability, high biocompatibility and excellent detection capabilities, further explores their future applicability in biomedical field and clinical diagnosis.
None.

One of the authors (Rajkumar Bandi) gratefully acknowledges CSIR-INDIA for Senior Research Fellowship. The authors are also grateful to MRC, MNIT Jaipur for extending various analytical facilities. The authors would like to thank DST-FIST, New Delhi, India for providing necessary analytical facilities in the department. Sincere thanks to Department of Chemistry, Osmania University (OU) and CFRD-OU for providing infrastructure and other necessary facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2017.11.010.

Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
Improving the functionality of carbon nanodots: doping and surface functionalization
Carbon dots: surface engineering and applications
